content.text: Debugging in production for agentic AI systems is the discipline of diagnosing and repairing failures in AI agents that are actively running in live environments — handling real user requests, executing real tool calls, and producing outputs that have real consequences. It is fundamentally different from traditional software debugging because the failure modes are unique: a single agent run may span dozens of LLM calls, interleaved tool invocations, and stateful context that evolves over many turns, none of which can be stepped through in a conventional debugger.

An agent failure in production rarely manifests as a clean exception with a stack trace. Instead it appears as an unhelpful final answer, a tool call with a hallucinated argument, a context window overflow, a cost spike from a runaway loop, or a silent non-answer returned to the user. The root cause may lie in the system prompt, in how a tool result was formatted back to the model, in a state management bug, or in unexpected model behavior after a model version upgrade.

Production debugging for agents therefore requires a layered observability strategy: every step the agent takes must be instrumented before it executes, so that when a failure occurs you have a complete record of every decision the system made, every prompt it sent, every response it received, every tool it invoked, and every piece of state it carried forward.

Traditional software has deterministic, reproducible execution: given the same inputs the program always produces the same outputs, and you can replay a bug locally with a debugger attached. Agentic AI systems break this contract on two fronts. First, LLM inference is stochastic — the same prompt can produce different outputs on different calls, and temperature, model version, and system load all affect results. Second, agents are typically long-running and stateful, meaning the prompt sent on turn four depends on the outputs of turns one through three. Reproducing a production failure requires reconstructing that entire accumulated context, not just the final input.

The stakes are also higher. An agent that files tickets, sends emails, modifies databases, or makes API calls on behalf of users can cause irreversible harm if it misbehaves. A bug that causes a standard web server to return a 500 error is annoying; a bug that causes an agent to send an email to the wrong recipient, delete a production record, or exfiltrate sensitive data to an external API is a serious incident. The ability to understand exactly what happened — and to prevent it from happening again — is a safety-critical capability.

Additionally, agent systems are expensive to run. Each LLM call consumes tokens, and a looping or confused agent can run up thousands of dollars in API costs before anyone notices. Production debugging infrastructure that includes cost tracking and anomaly alerting is not optional — it is a cost-control mechanism.
A complete trace of every step — with prompt, response, tool name, tool input, and tool output — reduces the time to find a bug from hours of guesswork to minutes of structured trace inspection.
Saving the full message history and tool outputs from a failed run enables a developer to replay that exact scenario locally, iterate on a fix, and validate it without touching production state.
Per-run token accounting surfaces which agent flows are expensive and enables budget-based alerting before a runaway run accumulates a large bill.
Saving failed-run traces as test fixtures lets the team add them to the regression test suite. A fix for a production bug becomes a permanent guard against that bug recurring.

Analyzing aggregated traces reveals systematic prompt weaknesses — for instance, steps where the model consistently misunderstands a tool's return format — that can be fixed before they cause user-facing failures.
A complete immutable log of every action an agent took is a compliance requirement in regulated industries. If a user questions what the agent did on their behalf, the trace is the evidence.

Comprehensive instrumentation has real costs. Logging every prompt and response means storing potentially large volumes of text — a 100-turn agent run at 2,000 tokens per turn generates 200,000 tokens of data per run. At scale, this adds non-trivial storage cost and latency.

Even with the exact same prompt and context, an LLM may return a different response in a replay. You can reproduce the scenario but not guarantee bit-for-bit identical model behavior. Use temperature=0 and seeded sampling where the model provider supports it.
User inputs often contain personal data. Logging full prompts may violate GDPR, HIPAA, or internal data policies. Implement PII redaction before traces are written to long-term storage.
Synchronous span export can add latency to the agent's hot path. Use asynchronous, batched export (the OTLP exporter's default) so instrumentation does not slow down LLM calls.

In multi-agent systems, a failure in a subagent may only become visible when the orchestrator receives a bad result. Trace IDs must propagate across agent boundaries to correlate the full causal chain.
Root cause in the model, not the code.

Sometimes the agent code is correct but the model is wrong. If a model version upgrade changes behavior, the fix is a prompt adjustment or model rollback, not a code change. Tracing helps identify this class of failure, but fixing it is a different skill.
Production debugging for agentic AI rests on three complementary pillars:
. Together they answer three questions: what happened (logging), when and in what order (tracing), and why it fails (replay).
to each agent invocation at entry and propagating it through every downstream call — each LLM request, each tool call, each subagent spawn. Every unit of work within the run is recorded as a

with a start time, end time, and structured attributes (model name, token counts, tool name, input, output). The spans form a tree that can be rendered as a waterfall, making it easy to see the sequence of operations and identify where time was spent or where errors occurred.
complements tracing by recording the full content of prompts and responses as JSON objects that include the
as a field. This means you can search logs by trace ID and retrieve every prompt, response, tool input, and tool output from a specific run — the complete conversation history the model actually saw.

attrs.alt: Agentic AI production debugging architecture: Production Agent (orchestrator, LLM calls, tool calls, memory, errors, spans) emits to Observability Layer (tracing, logging, metrics, alerting, replay store, cost tracking), which feeds Debug and Remediation tools (trace inspector, log query, offline replay, prompt diff, incident response flow, human-in-the-loop gate, guardrail injection).

content.text: The architecture has three zones. The

(left, blue) is the live system: an orchestrator routing requests through LLM calls, tool calls, and a stateful context. Every step emits a span and a structured log entry tagged with the run's trace ID. The
(center, green) collects those signals: tracing infrastructure (via OpenTelemetry), a log aggregator, a metrics database, an alerting system, a replay store, and cost tracking. The

zone (right, purple) is the tooling and process layer: a trace inspector UI for waterfall views, a log query interface for full-text prompt search, an offline replay harness for local reproduction, prompt diff tooling for comparing prompt versions across deploys, a structured incident response flow, a human-in-the-loop gate for pausing risky actions, and guardrail injection for blocking dangerous tool calls.

Production debugging infrastructure should be in place before an agent goes live, not after the first incident. The observability stack cannot be bolted on retroactively — by definition, the failure that needs debugging has already happened and the data you needed was not recorded.
Use full tracing from day one
whenever an agent executes actions with side effects — writing data, calling external APIs, sending messages.
during any production rollout of a new agent or a prompt update. This is the fastest way to catch regressions that only appear on real user inputs.
when investigating a specific failure: load the trace, reconstruct the message history, iterate on a fix locally before pushing to production.
Set a per-run and per-hour budget alarm in the agent's early days. Runaway loops are common during development and early rollout.
whenever the agent has write permissions to critical systems (production databases, billing APIs, user communications) until failure rates are proven low enough to trust fully automated execution.
Assign a trace ID at entry.
Generate a UUID at the top of every agent invocation. Attach it to a context variable or pass it explicitly through every function call. This is the linking key for all downstream data.

Wrap the LLM client in a function that opens a span before the call, records model, messages (or a hash for PII-sensitive runs), token counts from the response, stop reason, and latency, then closes the span whether the call succeeds or fails.

Wrap tool functions in the same way: open a span with the tool name, log the input arguments and the returned value (or the exception), record duration. Tool calls are a common failure point and need their own spans, not just a note in a surrounding LLM span.
After each turn, append the current
list to a replay store keyed by trace ID. A simple approach is writing a JSON file per run; a more robust approach is a database keyed by
Configure alerts on: error rate above threshold, per-run cost above budget, agent loop depth above maximum, and any uncaught exception. Route alerts to an on-call channel.

Write a CLI or script that accepts a trace ID, loads the saved message history and tool outputs, and re-runs the agent with those inputs but against the current (possibly patched) code. This script is what developers use to verify a fix before deploying.
Integrate with an LLM observability platform.

Tools like Langfuse, LangSmith, or Phoenix provide out-of-the-box trace viewers, session dashboards, and prompt version comparison. Using one reduces the amount of custom instrumentation you need to write.
Write a runbook that specifies: where to find traces, how to load a replay, how to roll back a prompt version, and when to escalate. This should exist before the first incident.
Structured logging with trace ID propagation

# Minimal structured logging harness for an agentic system
# pip install anthropic structlog

import uuid
import time
import structlog
import anthropic

log = structlog.get_logger()


def create_run_logger(trace_id: str):
    """Return a logger bound to a specific trace ID."""
    return log.bind(trace_id=trace_id, service="my-agent")


def call_llm(client: anthropic.Anthropic, messages: list, trace_id: str, turn: int) -> anthropic.types.Message:
    """Wrap an Anthropic API call with structured logging."""
    run_log = log.bind(trace_id=trace_id, turn=turn)
    run_log.info("llm_call_start", model="claude-sonnet-4-5", message_count=len(messages))

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=messages,
        )
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
        run_log.info(
            "llm_call_end",
            stop_reason=response.stop_reason,
            input_tokens=response.usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=response.usage.output_tokens,
            latency_s=round(elapsed, 3),
        )
        return response
    except Exception as exc:
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
        run_log.error("llm_call_error", error=str(exc), latency_s=round(elapsed, 3))
        raise


def call_tool(tool_name: str, tool_input: dict, trace_id: str) -> dict:
    """Wrap a tool invocation with structured logging."""
    run_log = log.bind(trace_id=trace_id, tool=tool_name)
    run_log.info("tool_call_start", input=tool_input)

    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    try:
        # Dispatch to actual tool implementation
        result = dispatch_tool(tool_name, tool_input)
        run_log.info("tool_call_end", output=result, latency_s=round(time.perf_counter() - t0, 3))
        return result
    except Exception as exc:
        run_log.error("tool_call_error", error=str(exc), latency_s=round(time.perf_counter() - t0, 3))
        raise


def run_agent(user_message: str) -> str:
    trace_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    run_log = create_run_logger(trace_id)
    run_log.info("agent_run_start", user_message=user_message)

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    for turn in range(10):  # max 10 turns
        response = call_llm(client, messages, trace_id, turn)

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            final = response.content[0].text
            run_log.info("agent_run_end", turns=turn + 1, final_answer_length=len(final))
            return final

        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            tool_results = []
            for block in response.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    result = call_tool(block.name, block.input, trace_id)
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": str(result),
                    })
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    run_log.error("agent_run_exceeded_max_turns", max_turns=10)
    raise RuntimeError("Agent exceeded maximum turn limit")


def dispatch_tool(name: str, inp: dict) -> dict:
    """Stub: real implementation would route to actual tool functions."""
    return {"result": f"stub output for {name}"}

# OpenTelemetry tracing for agentic AI
# pip install opentelemetry-sdk opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc anthropic

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
import anthropic

# --- One-time setup (call at process startup) ---
def configure_tracing(otlp_endpoint: str = "http://localhost:4317"):
    provider = TracerProvider()
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=otlp_endpoint)
    provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)

configure_tracing()
tracer = trace.get_tracer("my-agent")

# --- Instrumented agent entry point ---
def run_agent_traced(user_message: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.run") as root_span:
        root_span.set_attribute("agent.user_message_length", len(user_message))

        client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
        total_input_tokens = 0
        total_output_tokens = 0

        for turn in range(10):
            with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent.llm_call") as llm_span:
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.turn", turn)
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.model", "claude-sonnet-4-5")
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.message_count", len(messages))

                response = client.messages.create(
                    model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
                    max_tokens=1024,
                    messages=messages,
                )
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.stop_reason", response.stop_reason)
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.input_tokens", response.usage.input_tokens)
                llm_span.set_attribute("llm.output_tokens", response.usage.output_tokens)
                total_input_tokens += response.usage.input_tokens
                total_output_tokens += response.usage.output_tokens

            if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
                root_span.set_attribute("agent.turns", turn + 1)
                root_span.set_attribute("agent.total_input_tokens", total_input_tokens)
                root_span.set_attribute("agent.total_output_tokens", total_output_tokens)
                return response.content[0].text

            if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
                messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
                tool_results = []
                for block in response.content:
                    if block.type == "tool_use":
                        with tracer.start_as_current_span(f"agent.tool.{block.name}") as tool_span:
                            tool_span.set_attribute("tool.name", block.name)
                            tool_span.set_attribute("tool.id", block.id)
                            result = dispatch_tool(block.name, block.input)
                            tool_span.set_attribute("tool.success", True)
                        tool_results.append({
                            "type": "tool_result",
                            "tool_use_id": block.id,
                            "content": str(result),
                        })
                messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

        root_span.set_attribute("agent.error", "max_turns_exceeded")
        raise RuntimeError("Agent exceeded maximum turn limit")


def dispatch_tool(name: str, inp: dict) -> dict:
    return {"result": f"stub output for {name}"}
Consider a customer-support agent that handles billing inquiries. It has access to three tools:
. On a Thursday morning the alerting system fires: error rate on
tool calls has jumped from 2% to 34% in the last hour. Here is the complete incident response using the production debugging infrastructure.

# --- incident_replay.py ---
# Offline replay harness for the billing support agent incident.
# Usage: python incident_replay.py --trace-id <uuid>

import argparse
import json
from pathlib import Path
import anthropic


def load_run_state(trace_id: str) -> dict:
    """Load the persisted run state from the replay store."""
    # In production this hits a database; here we load from a file the
    # observability layer wrote during the failing run.
    path = Path(f"replay_store/{trace_id}.json")
    return json.loads(path.read_text())


def stub_refund_tool(account_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> dict:
    """
    Deterministic stub that returns the same error the prod tool returned.
    Loaded from the saved tool_results in the run state.
    """
    # During the incident, the billing API began returning 422 when
    # amount_cents was 0, which happened because the invoice lookup
    # returned None for a voided invoice and the agent passed 0.
    if amount_cents == 0:
        return {"error": "invalid_amount", "code": 422, "message": "Refund amount must be positive"}
    return {"refund_id": f"ref_{account_id[:6]}", "status": "pending"}


TOOLS = {
    "lookup_account": lambda **kw: {"account_id": "acct_123", "plan": "pro"},
    "get_invoice_history": lambda **kw: [
        {"invoice_id": "inv_001", "amount_cents": None, "status": "voided"},
    ],
    "issue_refund": lambda account_id, amount_cents, **kw: stub_refund_tool(account_id, amount_cents),
}


def replay_from_failure_point(trace_id: str, apply_fix: bool = False):
    """
    Replay the agent starting from the turn where the error occurred.
    Set apply_fix=True to test a proposed fix before deploying it.
    """
    state = load_run_state(trace_id)
    messages = state["messages_at_failure"]  # saved snapshot

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Optionally inject a fixed system prompt
    system = state["system_prompt"]
    if apply_fix:
        # FIX: instruct the agent to skip refunds on voided invoices
        system += "\n\nIMPORTANT: Never call issue_refund if the invoice status is 'voided'. " \
                  "Inform the user instead."

    print(f"Replaying trace {trace_id} (fix_applied={apply_fix})")
    for turn in range(5):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            system=system,
            messages=messages,
        )
        print(f"  Turn {turn}: stop_reason={response.stop_reason}")

        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            print(f"  Final answer: {response.content[0].text[:120]}")
            return response.content[0].text

        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            tool_results = []
            for block in response.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    result = TOOLS[block.name](**block.input)
                    print(f"    Tool {block.name}: input={block.input} -> result={result}")
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": json.dumps(result),
                    })
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

    raise RuntimeError("Replay exceeded turn limit")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--trace-id", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--fix", action="store_true", help="Apply the proposed fix")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    # First: reproduce the bug
    print("=== Reproducing the original failure ===")
    try:
        replay_from_failure_point(args.trace_id, apply_fix=False)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  Error (expected): {e}")

    print()

    # Second: verify the fix
    if args.fix:
        print("=== Verifying the fix ===")
        result = replay_from_failure_point(args.trace_id, apply_fix=True)
        print(f"  Fix verified. Agent handled gracefully.")
immediately reproduces the failure: the billing API returns a 422 when
for a voided invoice's amount. The agent passed that
to the refund tool. Running with
confirms the prompt addition resolves the issue. The fix is deployed; the trace is saved as a regression test fixture.

Only logging when an exception is raised means you have no data about what happened in the turns before the failure. The exception at turn seven was almost certainly caused by something that occurred at turn three. Log every turn, every LLM call, and every tool invocation — success or failure — at the
Missing trace ID in tool calls

The trace ID must appear in every log line, including those emitted by tool implementations. If the tool logs with its own logger that does not include the trace ID, the data from the tool and the data from the agent are siloed. Pass the trace ID explicitly to every instrumented function, or use a context-local variable (
in Python) that can be read by any logger in the same async context.
No upper bound on agent loops
An agent with no maximum turn limit will loop indefinitely if it enters a confused state — repeatedly calling the same tool, never reaching a final answer. Every agent must have a
guard that raises an exception and logs a
event after which the alert fires. Without this, a single user session can exhaust an API quota.
Replay that doesn't isolate tool side-effects
A replay harness that calls the real

tool during debugging will issue real refunds. Every tool with side effects — writes, sends, deletes — must be replaced with a stub during replay. Clearly separate replay-safe stubs from production tool implementations and enforce this via code review.
Confusing model errors with code errors

When a model returns a malformed tool call or an unexpected response, the natural impulse is to look for a code bug. Sometimes the code is correct and the model is the variable: a recent model upgrade changed behavior, or the model is producing valid JSON that does not match the tool's expected schema. Log the raw response content (not just the parsed form) so you can distinguish between a parsing failure and a malformed model output.
Add tracing, logging, and alerting before the first production deployment. Retrofitting after an incident under pressure leads to gaps and mistakes.
Use one trace ID per user-initiated run.
The trace ID should be generated once at the entry point and propagated through every downstream call — LLM, tool, subagent. Never generate a new trace ID mid-run.
Log the full messages list, not just the last turn.
The context that caused the failure often lives in earlier turns. Log or snapshot the complete message history periodically so replay is possible from any point.
Set a budget per run and per hour.
Track token counts per span, accumulate them, and compare against a per-run budget. Emit a warning event when a run exceeds the expected cost and an error event when it exceeds the hard limit.
Save failed traces as regression tests.

Every production incident should produce a fixture that is added to the test suite. The combination of the saved message history and the expected corrected behavior is a permanent guard against the bug recurring.
Actions that cannot be undone — sending emails, issuing refunds, modifying production records — should pause and require explicit operator confirmation during the initial rollout phase.
) in every span. If behavior changes after an update, filtering traces by model version immediately isolates the affected population.
A well-implemented observability stack adds negligible latency to the agent's hot path. The two performance-sensitive areas are span export and log writes. Both should be asynchronous and batched.
accumulates spans in memory and flushes them on a schedule or when the buffer is full. This decouples trace export from LLM call latency entirely.
Avoid synchronous disk I/O in the critical path.
If you write the replay store to disk, do it in a background thread or async task. The time to serialize and write a 50KB JSON file to a slow disk can add tens of milliseconds.

If an agent handles thousands of runs per minute, logging full prompts for every run is expensive. Use trace sampling — log 100% of errors and a configurable percentage of successful runs (5–10% is often sufficient for monitoring purposes).
Compress and TTL the replay store.

JSON message histories compress 5–10x with gzip. Store compressed traces with a time-to-live (30–90 days is typical) to prevent unbounded storage growth. Keep failed traces longer than successful ones.
Separate observability backends from the agent's critical path.
If the trace backend is down, the agent should continue running. Use a circuit breaker around trace export so an OTLP collector outage does not cause agent failures.
Open source, self-hostable, session traces, prompt versioning, evals
Teams that want full control over their trace data
Tight LangChain integration, dataset creation from traces, eval harness
Local-first, rich trace viewer, built-in evals, retrieval quality metrics
RAG pipelines and local development debugging
Vendor-neutral, rich SDK, compatible with Jaeger/Zipkin/Grafana Tempo
Unified logs/metrics/traces, LLM Observability product, alerting
Enterprises already using Datadog for other services
Self-hosted, correlated logs/traces/metrics, cost-effective at scale
Structured JSON output, context binding, processor pipeline, stdlib compatible
Any Python agent needing production-grade structured logging
Agents with side effects in production
Good starting point; pair with OTel for infra traces

The observability stack itself is a high-value attack target and a potential data leak. Prompt and response logs may contain user PII, internal business data, API keys accidentally included in messages, or information about the agent's tool schemas and capabilities. Treat the trace and log store with the same sensitivity as your application database.

Use a PII detection library (e.g., Microsoft Presidio, AWS Comprehend PII) to scrub emails, phone numbers, and other identifiers from prompt text before writing to long-term storage. Log a hash or placeholder instead.
Only engineers who need to debug production incidents should have read access to the replay store and full prompt logs. Use role-based access control and audit log all access to trace data.
Tool input logging may accidentally capture API keys passed by the model. Implement a blocklist of field names (
) that are redacted before any tool input is written to logs.

The replay script loads message histories from storage and executes code. Ensure it cannot load malicious content from untrusted traces. Validate the schema of loaded state before passing it to the model.
Degrade gracefully when observability is unavailable.

If the trace backend or log aggregator is unreachable, the agent should continue serving requests, not fail. Wrap all observability calls in try/except blocks and never propagate observability errors to the caller.
Apply TTL and access controls to replay data.
Set automated expiry (30–90 days) on replay store entries. Ensure the data is encrypted at rest. Store only as much as needed for debugging, not indefinitely.
Agentic AI failures are not traditional bugs: they span multiple LLM turns and tool calls, and the root cause is often in accumulated context or model behavior rather than a single line of code.
Every agent invocation must generate a single trace ID that links all spans — LLM calls, tool calls, subagent spawns — into a coherent causal chain that can be inspected after a failure.

Persist the complete message history for every run in a replay store. The ability to reproduce a production failure locally — without triggering real tool side effects — is the foundation of fast incident resolution.
Gate irreversible actions (writes, sends, deletes) behind human approval and add a hard
limit with alerting to prevent runaway loops from accumulating API costs or causing unintended side effects.
Save every production failure trace as a test fixture. Fixed bugs become regression tests, permanently guarding against recurrence.
Treat the observability data store with the same security posture as the production database: apply PII redaction before writing, use RBAC, set data TTLs, and encrypt at rest.
Purpose-built LLM observability platforms (Langfuse, LangSmith, Phoenix) reduce the instrumentation burden significantly. Start with one rather than building a custom trace viewer.
